g.data-narration: pclose() is essential for proper resource management - it closes pipes and waits for child processes to prevent zombie processes.
popen() creates pipes and spawns shell commands, while pclose() closes them and waits for process termination with exit status.
popen() takes command string and type ('r' or 'w'), returning a FILE pointer. pclose() takes the stream and returns exit status.
Internally, popen() creates a pipe with pipe(), forks a child process, and redirects standard input or output to the pipe.
g.text: Returns: FILE pointer connected to pipe (parent reads child's stdout OR writes to child's stdin)

g.data-narration: popen() creates unidirectional pipes only. Type 'r' lets you read command output, type 'w' lets you write to command input.
g.text: ⚠ Note: popen() creates only unidirectional pipes. Bidirectional requires two popen() calls.

g.data-narration: pclose() performs four operations: closes the FILE stream, calls waitpid() to wait for child, collects exit status, and handles errors.
Use WIFEXITED to check normal exit, WEXITSTATUS for exit code, WIFSIGNALED for signal termination, WTERMSIG for signal number.
This example shows reading command output using popen with type 'r', reading lines with fgets, and checking exit status with pclose.
This example demonstrates writing to a command (bc calculator) using type 'w', sending expressions via fprintf, and closing properly.
g.text: FILE *fp = popen("bc -q", "w"); // Open pipe for writing
pclose(fp); // Closes input, waits for bc to finish

g.data-narration: This comprehensive example shows proper error handling: checking popen return, handling pclose errors, and examining both normal and signal termination.
Avoid these common mistakes: forgetting pclose creates zombies, not checking popen return causes crashes, and single popen can't do bidirectional.
Follow best practices: always match popen with pclose, check return values, use fflush before pclose, and understand pclose blocks until child exits.
Key takeaways: popen creates unidirectional pipes, pclose closes and collects exit status, always check for errors, and prevent resource leaks.
g.text: • popen() creates pipes + spawns shell command
• pclose() closes pipe + waits for child + returns status
• Use WIFEXITED/WEXITSTATUS to check exit code

g.data-narration: Quick reference: include stdio.h and sys/wait.h, remember function prototypes, and always properly close pipes to prevent system resource leaks.
g.text: ← Study tip: Practice with ls, grep, bc commands →
